speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred Winter on July 11, 1996, in Norwalk, Connecticut. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you in Nuremberg, staying-- excuse me-- amongst the German people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed with your fiancee?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got-- where the Americans were housed, she got a room for me in that building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become friendly with any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the trials affect your relationships with German civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had very little contact, even in the hometown, when I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any antisemitism in the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with your family in Sweden while you were in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what news did you receive about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were doing well, and they had jobs in the meantime in Sweden. And they were hoping that I would be able to come to Sweden soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all through letters. You had no visits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But [? also ?] I had a letter from Sweden that they wanted to come back to Germany. And I wrote them to Sweden, you may be able to come back to Germany, but I will not come back to Germany because no Jew has business in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- what do you think should be the legacy of the Nuremberg Trials? What should the world know from those trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: That you cannot get away with murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the Nuremberg Trials have affected history and our events as human beings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, they are not teaching enough about the Nuremberg Trials. They should teach a lot more about the Nuremberg Trials and the Holocaust. The Germans have suppressed that. So have the Austrians. The French have suppressed it because the French were involved also, quite a bit. And the British have not shown their best face because there was the trouble in-- in Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- more or less, I have to blame the world as it is today for the trouble which we have as a world today, what we have in-- in-- in former Yugoslavia, what's going on in Africa, and everywhere. Because we have too many who are, let's say, don't give a damn. When the trial ended, where you and your fiancee go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- I left Germany before the trial was to end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were reunited with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who had survived of all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except for my stepmother, everyone-- or my brother, everyone had survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, from Sweden, how did you get your fiancee from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went back to England, and then she applied for a visitor's visa to Sweden. Then she got a visitor's visa-- visa to Sweden. When she came over, we got married. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was the wedding?

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your entire family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only your father. After the wedding, where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [? Liarum. ?] That's near [? Gothenburg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till March 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had planned, when we were in Germany, that we would go to the United States because she had an uncle here in the United States, her mother's brother, who had told us that he would support her to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, where did you settle first?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Springfield, Mass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you work as during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned to work in a textile factory in Sweden, and they gave me training as a supervisor. On a [INAUDIBLE], and I worked for Johnson Johnson. And instead of a regular factory worker, I was a utility worker-- utility and layout. So I was making more money than a regular factory worker was making already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other jobs have you had after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to school to learn English. I learned some English in Germany, but not enough-- [? not. ?] When I managed English, I applied to a factory, and I became a supervisor in shipping, receiving, and inspection. And I stayed with that company for 12 years. And I had a little argument with the company president. He was not too honest, and I left. And I went to [? Spalding, ?] and I became supervisor in quality control.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children, you and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 15, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1951. And did he go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school and college.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What college did he go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: American International College in Springfield, Mass, first, and then University of Connecticut in Storrs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Connecticut?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1981, when I got married second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about what happened with your first wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: She died of cancer in December, 1974.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a relation between-- did your wives know one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were-- my second wife here, her father's mother was married to my father's sister. So I knew her, let's say, since she is out of diapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've known her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had contact after the war with her mother on [INAUDIBLE]. We came together. They came to Springfield. We came to Norwalk. So we know each even when my wife was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were going to leave a message to your children and your grandchildren, these 50 years since the Shoah, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never forget what happened, and teach everyone to be tolerant.

speaker: interviewer
text: While we're talking about tolerance, I think you were in other trials after Nuremberg. Would you like to tell me about those trials and how you heard about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was reading the which is a-- a Ger-- a German Jewish newspaper from New York, that they were looking for witnesses against certain people. And I contacted the prosecutor in Hamburg. And I had to go to the German consulate in Boston during that time and gave them an affidavit. But out in the consulate, they were not too friendly. He must have been an ex-Nazi. I had a few arguments over there. But maybe four or six weeks after, I got the sworn affidavit in the consulate. I had a letter from the prosecutor, and he asked me if I am willing to come to Hamburg to testify.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: To testify.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1970. And this was the worst trial I ever was in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- you went by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My wife had cancer. She could not travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it the worst trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I got treated like-- as if I were the defendant, as if I were the criminal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone meet you when you arrived in Hamburg or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Provide for your hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the arrangements were made by the prosecutor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel uncomfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no contact with the Germans, so I-- it was a Norwegian hotel. So very few Germans I had contact with, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this trial was about Ghetto Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Tuchel and Neumann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the outcome of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuchel got a life sentence. Neumann got 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you believe your testimony contributed to those sentences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially because I got the jury involved to ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: The jury involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the jury could-- well, women on a jury were flabbergasted when I told them that they molested women and-- and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remembered the details and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --recounted those details in court.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Hamburg again?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what trial was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maywald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also about Ghetto Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mostly about Salaspils where he took the watches and the wedding bands and everything. Also he killed somebody in Riga, but the Germans claim he did not because he was already in Minsk. And I say he may have been in Minsk, but he still killed somebody in Riga. We had an argument, but I didn't make any headway because I had no corroborating witness, because there is no survivor of the motor pool.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you gave this testimony, do you feel that it was an important contribution to the conviction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the sentence?

speaker: interviewee
text: He only got a five-year suspended sentence, because the court was, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't give a damn if the trial was or not. The prosecutor tried his best, but he got [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] treated like a dog by the judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your sense of the proceedings of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back one more time?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that trial about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wiesner, who had-- was in charge of the prison hospital in [INAUDIBLE] concentration camp Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that trial held?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dusseldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a witness for the prosecution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the outcome of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years prison because I did not see him kill somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was all you witnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only witnessed that somebody got beaten up and that some bodies were carried out of the infirmary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each time, when you returned from these trials, how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first trial, I was very satisfied even though I got treated like a dog in the courtroom. The other trials, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the years went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Especially in 1980, in the Maywald trial, I felt like I have been hit in the face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet you came back and went on with your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that we didn't ask you that you'd like to add? Any message? Why do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's a message--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That all Jews, Orthodox, Reform, or Conservative, or what-- doesn't matter from which country they come, should stick together and not fight each other like they did at the concentration camp. The Latvian Jews hated the German Jews. The German Jews hated the Poles. The Hungarian one didn't want to have to do anything with the-- the Polish Jews, especially those from Galicia. There were enemies in the camps, even, [? not ?] Jews or prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you personally think you survived, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was fighting back. And I swore myself, if they don't put a bullet in my head or a rope around my neck, I will see them hanging. And I never gave up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that all the acts of sabotage that you committed in the PX were part of the reason you had strength?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I had also good leaders who were-- were more aggressive than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leaders among the Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. About the 25 [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want the world to remember what happened, so that the world never will forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the way you lived your life, and why is that important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I'm in the United States, I had what you can say the American dream. If my first wife didn't have any cancer and she died, I would have had the best life you can think of. I never financial worries, and I still don't have any today, even though I'm partially paralyzed with my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there been any decisions you've made in your life that was-- were influenced by your experience during the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To put any racisms on-- on-- aside and treat everyone as a human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the message you'd like to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, can you introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Edith born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Monchengladbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 3, 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you lived in Connecticut?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Connecticut since 1981. She lives in Connecticut since 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you both meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know each other from childhood, and we were together in the ghetto of Riga, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children does Edith have?

speaker: interviewee
text: One son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you both have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years and one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Edith, is there anything you'd like to say on the tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't, because I don't hear it. Though I would like that he take it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Do you think Edith would like to give a message from both of you to your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to [INAUDIBLE] remember what happened in the past and make sure it never will happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us where all your family ended up after Sweden? Your father came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States. My youngest brother came to the United States. My sister in the Dominican Republic, and my sister-in-law was in Colombia. Now she lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your twin brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got killed in May 1945, against the Japanese and the French army in Indochina.

speaker: interviewer
text: A last message for the world? Would you like to say one last thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget what happened. When we tell around to everyone, though it will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, can you tell us the year of the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know exactly when the photo was made. But this is my father and seven brothers who were soldiers in the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Imperial Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius Winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the names of the brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Albert Winter, Morris, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Abraham, Louis, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gustav, and Ziegfried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are parents from Edith. That's Alfred and [? Frieda ?] Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are we?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Monchengladbach, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, Julius and Henrietta Winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the picture for a special occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, can you tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is Edith Rosen, now my wife. That's [? Kurt ?] [GERMAN], which was her fiance. That's a friend, [? Ilse ?] [GERMAN], my brother Kurt Winter, and Hilde-- Hilde Zander, who became his wife later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August. In Monchengladbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the special occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith's birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you taken soon after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months later, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sister-in-law, Hilde, and the picture was taken in 1946, in Cali, Colombia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she play a role in your work assignments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she influenced [INAUDIBLE] that we would have good work commandos so that we would stay alive in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, can you tell us about this proclamation?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document from Theresienstadt that I was a group leader in a crematoria when they were fighting the typhus epidemic till July 11, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started in April.

speaker: interviewee
text: And started in April there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what my sister-in-law wrote after the war, when we were together in the ghetto of Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilde Sherman, now. She was Hilde Winter. She was the wife of my brother who died in Salaspils.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the book released?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- and middle of '70s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my father. It was taken in 1945, after the liberation, in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Edith, and myself. The picture was taken probably in 1987, '88.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son and his wife, Frances. They got married in 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: John.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Silver Spring, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Fred and Sandra Stern. Fred is Edith's son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK. There's my son, John, and his wife, Frances. That is Sandra and Alfred Stern. That's the son from Edith. That's Edith and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the wedding of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding of Alfred Stern and Sandra-- and Sandra [? Sternbaum. ?] The wedding was in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry. I do this professionally. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Holden and Hunter Stern, our grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Monroe, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holden is four and Hunter is one year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
